questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The practice of seed saving—collecting and storing seeds from one harvest to plant the next—has sustained agriculture for over 10,000 years. In the twentieth century, however, the rise of commercial seed companies and hybrid varieties led to a dramatic decline in the genetic diversity of crops. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization, roughly 75% of the world's crop diversity was lost between 1900 and 2000. Today, a network of community seed banks and exchanges has emerged to preserve heirloom varieties, arguing that this genetic library is not a nostalgic indulgence but an essential insurance policy against future pests, diseases, and climate disruption.

What is the main idea of the passage?
options: Seed saving is an outdated practice with no modern relevance.
Crop diversity has declined sharply due to commercial agriculture, but community seed banks are working to preserve it as a safeguard for the future.
Hybrid seeds are superior to heirloom varieties in all respects.
The FAO is primarily responsible for seed preservation globally.
questions.explanation: The passage traces the loss of crop diversity due to commercial agriculture and describes the emergence of community seed banks as a 'genetic library' and 'essential insurance policy.' Option B captures both the problem and the solution. Options A and C contradict the passage, and D misattributes responsibility.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The practice of seed saving—collecting and storing seeds from one harvest to plant the next—has sustained agriculture for over 10,000 years. In the twentieth century, however, the rise of commercial seed companies and hybrid varieties led to a dramatic decline in the genetic diversity of crops. According to the Food and Agriculture Organization, roughly 75% of the world's crop diversity was lost between 1900 and 2000. Today, a network of community seed banks and exchanges has emerged to preserve heirloom varieties, arguing that this genetic library is not a nostalgic indulgence but an essential insurance policy against future pests, diseases, and climate disruption.

Which detail best supports the claim that crop diversity has declined significantly?
options: Seed saving has been practiced for over 10,000 years.
The rise of commercial seed companies occurred in the twentieth century.
Roughly 75% of the world's crop diversity was lost between 1900 and 2000, according to the FAO.
Heirloom varieties are preserved in community seed banks.
questions.explanation: The claim of significant decline is directly supported by the FAO statistic that 75% of crop diversity was lost. Option C provides this specific, quantitative evidence. Options A and B are context, and D describes a response to the decline, not evidence of it.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
Choreographer Alvin Ailey's 1960 masterpiece *Revelations* draws on his memories of growing up in the Black church in rural Texas. But dance historian Thomas DeFrantz argues that to read the work as purely autobiographical is to miss its broader ambition. Ailey, he contends, was not simply telling his own story; he was creating what he called 'blood memories'—a choreographic language that could express the collective grief, resilience, and joy of the African American experience across generations.

Which title best captures DeFrantz's argument?
options: A Biography of Alvin Ailey
Beyond Autobiography: How *Revelations* Expresses Collective Memory
A Technical Guide to Modern Dance
The History of the Black Church in Texas
questions.explanation: DeFrantz argues *Revelations* is not 'purely autobiographical' but expresses a 'collective' experience. Option B captures this argument of moving beyond the personal to the communal. Option A is too broad and misses the specific argument about the work's meaning.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
Marine biologist Dr. Hana Kim argues that the recovery of sea otter populations along the California coast has had an unexpected benefit: the restoration of seagrass beds. She presents data showing that by preying on crabs, which in turn eat the sea slugs that clean algae from seagrass, otters indirectly protect the seagrass from being smothered.

Which sentence provides the strongest textual evidence for Kim's claim?
options: Sea otter populations have recovered along the California coast.
Seagrass beds are important marine habitats.
By eating crabs, otters allow sea slugs to thrive and clean algae from seagrass, preventing the beds from being smothered.
Dr. Hana Kim is a marine biologist.
questions.explanation: Kim's claim is that otters indirectly benefit seagrass through a trophic cascade. Option C provides the full causal chain—otter-crab-slug-algae-seagrass—which is the specific evidence for the unexpected benefit. Options A and B are context.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A dermatologist claims that daily sunscreen use significantly reduces visible signs of skin aging. Which of the following, if true, would provide the best evidence?
options: Sunscreen prevents sunburn.

A four-year study of 900 adults found that those who applied broad-spectrum sunscreen daily showed 24% less skin aging—measured by wrinkles, texture, and pigmentation—than those who used sunscreen only occasionally, as assessed by blinded dermatologists.
Many people forget to apply sunscreen daily.
Sunscreen is available in various SPF levels.
questions.explanation: The claim concerns visible skin aging. Option B provides the best evidence—a multi-year study with objective, blinded assessment showing a 24% reduction in measured aging signs. Options A, C, and D do not provide outcome evidence for the aging claim.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Planet} & \text{Orbital Period (Earth days)} \\ \hline \text{Mercury} & 88 \\ \hline \text{Venus} & 225 \\ \hline \text{Earth} & 365 \\ \hline \text{Mars} & 687 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
options: Mars has the longest orbital period among the four planets listed.
Venus is the hottest planet in the solar system.
All planets have similar orbital periods.
Mercury takes more than a year to orbit the Sun.
questions.explanation: Mars at 687 Earth days has the longest orbital period among the four listed. Option A is directly supported. Option B is about temperature, not in the data. C is false (88 to 687). D is false (88 days is less than a year).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A 2023 report claims that noise-canceling headphones improve concentration in open-plan offices. Researchers tested 200 workers with and without noise-canceling headphones.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Condition} & \text{Tasks Completed (per hour)} & \text{Self-Reported Distraction Level} \\ \hline \text{Noise-Canceling Headphones} & 8.4 & 2.1 \\ \hline \text{No Headphones (Control)} & 6.2 & 7.8 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data relate to the report's claim?
options: The data contradicts the claim because workers without headphones also completed tasks.
The data supports the claim by showing higher productivity and lower distraction levels with noise-canceling headphones compared to the control.
The data is irrelevant because it does not measure long-term health effects.
The data suggests that headphones decrease productivity.
questions.explanation: The report claims headphones improve concentration. The table supports this: tasks increased (8.4 vs. 6.2 per hour) and distraction dropped (2.1 vs. 7.8 on the scale). Option D is contradicted by the data.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A gym owner argues that offering free fitness classes to new members for the first month will increase long-term membership retention. Which of the following, if true, would most weaken this argument?
options: Free classes are popular with new members.

Data from a chain of gyms showed that members who joined during free-class promotions had a 30% higher cancellation rate after six months than those who joined at standard rates, suggesting that the promotion attracted customers less committed to long-term fitness habits.
Fitness classes can accommodate many participants.
Retention is a key metric for gym profitability.
questions.explanation: The argument claims free classes increase retention. Option B weakens this by presenting data showing the opposite: promotional joiners had higher cancellation rates, suggesting the promotion attracted less committed members. Options A and C do not address long-term retention.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The mural had been painted on the side of the community center in 1978, a riot of color and protest that had defined the neighborhood's identity for decades. When the building was sold to developers, the new owners announced plans to paint over it. The day before the painters were scheduled to arrive, a crowd gathered. They did not chain themselves to the wall or shout slogans. They simply stood, hundreds of them, holding up their phones, their flashlights trained on the mural like candles in a vigil.

What can reasonably be inferred about the crowd's response?
options: They were indifferent to the mural's fate.
They chose a quiet, collective act of witness rather than confrontation to express what the mural meant to them.
They intended to vandalize the building.
They had been paid by the developers to create a spectacle.
questions.explanation: The crowd did not protest or chain themselves; they held a silent vigil with phone flashlights—a 'quiet, collective act of witness.' This suggests they valued the mural and chose a poignant, non-confrontational way to honor it. Options A and C contradict the description.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The expansion of telemedicine has been hailed as a revolution in healthcare access, particularly for rural communities. But physician and health policy researcher Dr. Robert Chen urges a more measured assessment. Telemedicine, he notes, requires reliable broadband internet—something many rural areas still lack—and works best for follow-up consultations rather than initial diagnoses. Without addressing the digital divide, he argues, telemedicine risks widening the very disparities it promises to close.

Which best describes Chen's perspective on telemedicine?
options: He enthusiastically embraces it as a panacea for all healthcare access problems.
He sees its potential but cautions that without addressing the digital divide, it may worsen the inequities it aims to solve.
He believes telemedicine should be banned because it is ineffective.
He is indifferent to the expansion of telemedicine.
questions.explanation: Chen 'urges a more measured assessment,' noting benefits but warning that broadband gaps and diagnostic limitations mean it could 'widen the very disparities it promises to close.' Option B captures this balanced, cautionary perspective. Options A and C overstate.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The librarian had worked at the branch for forty-two years. On her last day, she did not give a speech or accept a plaque. She spent the morning shelving returns, ran the afternoon story hour as she always did, and at five o'clock, she turned off the lights in the children's section, straightened the 'New Releases' display, and locked the door behind her. She did not say goodbye to the building. The building, she felt, had already said goodbye to her.

Based on the passage, the librarian's final actions suggest that she:
options: Was eager to leave and start her retirement.
Expressed her relationship to the library through quiet, familiar rituals rather than formal ceremony.
Was angry at the library administration for not honoring her.
Forgot that it was her last day of work.
questions.explanation: The librarian declines ceremony and instead performs her regular tasks—shelving, story hour, turning off lights—as a ritual of closure. Her feeling that 'the building had already said goodbye' suggests an intimate, nonverbal farewell. Option B captures this. Options A and C lack support.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The passage introduces the concept of 'urban heat islands'—areas where cities are significantly warmer than surrounding rural areas. It then explains the causes: dark surfaces that absorb solar radiation, waste heat from buildings and vehicles, and the lack of vegetation. Next, it examines the health consequences of extreme heat, particularly for elderly and low-income residents. The passage concludes by evaluating strategies to mitigate urban heat, including cool roofs, green spaces, and reflective pavements.

Which best describes the overall text structure?
options: A personal narrative about a heat wave
Definition of a phenomenon, its causes, its consequences, and strategies for mitigation
An argument that cities should be abandoned during summer months
A comparison of temperatures in different cities
questions.explanation: The structure moves from definition to causes to consequences to solutions. Option B captures this phenomenon-causes-consequences-mitigation progression.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The defendant's alibi was ______, resting on the testimony of a single witness whose credibility the prosecution systematically dismantled during cross-examination.
options: ironclad
tenuous
unassailable
compelling
questions.explanation: The description of a single witness whose credibility was 'systematically dismantled' indicates the alibi was weak. 'Tenuous' means weak or insubstantial. 'Ironclad' and 'unassailable' mean strong (opposite), and 'compelling' means convincing.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The CEO's memo described the layoffs as a 'restructuring,' a term that for shareholders signaled strategic reorganization but that for employees carried a far more painful ______: the loss of jobs and livelihoods dressed in the language of corporate jargon.
options: definition
connotation
investment
acronym
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the neutral corporate meaning with the painful employee interpretation. 'Connotation' captures the emotional and experiential associations—'the loss of jobs and livelihoods.' 'Definition' is the literal meaning without the emotional weight.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
In physics, ______ is defined as the product of an object's mass and its velocity. It is a vector quantity, meaning it has both magnitude and direction, and is conserved in isolated systems according to the law of conservation of momentum.
options: momentum
inertia
force
acceleration
questions.explanation: The definition—product of mass and velocity, a conserved vector quantity—is 'momentum.' 'Inertia' is resistance to change in motion, 'force' is mass times acceleration, and 'acceleration' is rate of change of velocity.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The waiting room was a study in muted anxiety. A woman turned the pages of a magazine without looking at them. A man checked his phone, checked it again thirty seconds later, checked it a third time. The receptionist typed at her computer with the practiced blankness of someone who had learned long ago not to absorb the tension that gathered daily in these chairs.

Which best describes the tone?
options: Cheerful and energetic
Quietly tense, capturing the low-grade anxiety of waiting
Angry and confrontational
Warm and comforting
questions.explanation: Details like 'muted anxiety,' 'turned pages without looking,' compulsive phone-checking, and the receptionist's 'practiced blankness' create a tone of subdued tension and unease. Options A and D contradict this, and C is too strong.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— The Paris Catacombs, a network of underground ossuaries, hold the remains of over six million people.
— They were created in the late eighteenth century when the city's cemeteries became overcrowded and posed a public health risk.
— The bones were transferred from cemeteries to the tunnels over the course of several decades, often in ceremonial nighttime processions.
— Today, a portion of the catacombs is open to the public as a museum, attracting over 500,000 visitors annually.

Which choice most effectively combines the information?
options: The Paris Catacombs hold millions of remains and were created in the 1700s as a solution to overcrowded cemeteries, with the bones transferred in nighttime ceremonies over decades; today they draw over half a million visitors annually to their museum section.
The catacombs are in Paris and have bones.
Millions of people are buried in the catacombs.
The catacombs are open to the public.
questions.explanation: Option A synthesizes all four notes: the number of remains, the historical problem and solution, the ceremonial process of transfer, and the modern use as a popular museum. Options B, C, and D each capture only fragments.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
A public transit agency wants to persuade drivers to switch to the newly expanded light rail system. Surveys show that commuters value speed and reliability above all else. Which approach would best achieve this goal?
options: A brochure listing all light rail stations and their addresses.

A campaign comparing average commute times: driving takes 55 minutes during rush hour versus 32 minutes on the light rail, with trains arriving every 6 minutes and an on-time rate of 96%—alongside testimonials from former drivers who now use the time to read or work.
A history of light rail development in the region.
An environmental impact report on the benefits of public transit.
questions.explanation: The audience values speed and reliability. Option B directly addresses this with specific data (32 vs. 55 minutes, 6-minute frequency, 96% on-time) and adds the bonus of productive commute time. Options A, C, and D do not directly address the stated values.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The deep sea is a realm of crushing pressure and perpetual darkness, ______ hydrothermal vents on the ocean floor support thriving ecosystems powered not by sunlight but by chemical energy from Earth's interior.
options: whereas
therefore
in addition,
for instance,
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the inhospitable deep sea with the thriving vent ecosystems. 'Whereas' signals this opposition. 'Therefore' would imply the darkness causes the thriving ecosystems (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the research team had only a limited budget and a six-month timeline, they managed to collect data from over 1,200 participants across four continents.
options: Although
Because
Unless
If
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts limited resources with impressive results. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' would make the limitations the cause of success (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The farmer's market has become a Saturday morning institution, it draws shoppers from across the city with its fresh produce and artisanal goods.
options: NO CHANGE
institution, and it
institution; it
institution it
questions.explanation: The original contains a comma splice. Option C correctly uses a semicolon. Option B is acceptable but the semicolon more effectively conveys the relationship. Option D creates a fused sentence.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The scholarship is open to students pursuing degrees in three fields ______ engineering, nursing, and education.
options: fields: engineering,
fields; engineering,
fields, engineering,
fields—engineering,
questions.explanation: A colon correctly introduces a list after an independent clause. 'The scholarship is open to students pursuing degrees in three fields' is complete, and the list specifies them. A semicolon, comma, or em dash would be incorrect.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
______ the trail was well-marked and regularly maintained, hikers were advised to carry a map and compass, as sudden fog could reduce visibility to near zero in a matter of minutes.
options: Although
Because
Unless
If
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the well-marked trail with the precautionary advice. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship. 'Because' would make the good maintenance the reason for carrying a map (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The restoration of the historic theater, which involved painstaking work by skilled craftspeople from across the region, ______ been hailed as a model for how communities can preserve their architectural heritage.
options: have
has
were
are
questions.explanation: The subject is 'restoration,' singular. The interrupting clause modifies but does not change the number. The singular verb 'has' correctly agrees. Options A, C, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The novelist completed the manuscript after years of revisions, and when ______ finally submitted it to the publisher, the editor called it the best work of a distinguished career.
options: they
she
it
we
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'The novelist,' singular. Without additional gender specification, 'she' is used. 'They' is plural in formal agreement, 'it' is for non-human antecedents, and 'we' includes the reader.

questions.question:
(Module 1 English)
The three ______ recommendations for improving the park were presented to the city council, each addressing a different aspect of the community's concerns.
options: architects
architects'
architect's
architect
questions.explanation: The possessive form is needed to show the recommendations belong to the architects. With three architects (plural), the apostrophe goes after the 's': 'architects'.' Option A lacks possession, C is singular possessive, and D is singular without possession.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The Tuskegee syphilis study, conducted by the U.S. Public Health Service between 1932 and 1972, withheld treatment from Black men with syphilis in order to observe the progression of the disease—even after penicillin became the standard cure. This history is often presented as an aberration, a singular moral failure. But medical historian Susan Reverby's research reveals that the Tuskegee study was not an isolated incident but the most visible example of a broader pattern of medical experimentation on Black Americans. Drawing on archival records from teaching hospitals, state penitentiaries, and medical journals, Reverby documents dozens of experiments—some stretching back to the antebellum era—in which Black patients were subjected to painful procedures without consent, used as subjects for surgical practice, or denied treatment in ways that shaped both medical knowledge and racial hierarchy. The legacy of these experiments, Reverby argues, is not just historical; it is embedded in the present-day mistrust of medical institutions that continues to shape health outcomes in Black communities.

What is the main idea of the passage?
options: The Tuskegee study was the only instance of medical mistreatment of Black Americans.
Reverby argues that Tuskegee was part of a long, documented history of medical experimentation on Black Americans, a legacy that continues to affect health outcomes through institutional mistrust.
Penicillin was ineffective against syphilis.
The U.S. Public Health Service has been fully reformed.
questions.explanation: Reverby's research shows Tuskegee was not an aberration but part of a documented pattern stretching to the antebellum era, with continuing effects today. Option B captures this historical scope and ongoing impact. Options A and D contradict the passage, and C is false.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The Tuskegee syphilis study, conducted by the U.S. Public Health Service between 1932 and 1972, withheld treatment from Black men with syphilis in order to observe the progression of the disease—even after penicillin became the standard cure. This history is often presented as an aberration, a singular moral failure. But medical historian Susan Reverby's research reveals that the Tuskegee study was not an isolated incident but the most visible example of a broader pattern of medical experimentation on Black Americans. Drawing on archival records from teaching hospitals, state penitentiaries, and medical journals, Reverby documents dozens of experiments—some stretching back to the antebellum era—in which Black patients were subjected to painful procedures without consent, used as subjects for surgical practice, or denied treatment in ways that shaped both medical knowledge and racial hierarchy. The legacy of these experiments, Reverby argues, is not just historical; it is embedded in the present-day mistrust of medical institutions that continues to shape health outcomes in Black communities.

Which detail best supports the claim that the pattern of experimentation extends beyond Tuskegee?
options: The Tuskegee study lasted from 1932 to 1972.
Penicillin became the standard cure for syphilis.
Reverby documented dozens of experiments stretching back to the antebellum era involving Black patients subjected to procedures without consent.
Susan Reverby is a medical historian.
questions.explanation: The claim that the pattern extends beyond Tuskegee is directly supported by the evidence of 'dozens of experiments' documented across time, reaching back to the antebellum era. Option C provides this specific historical scope. Options A and B are about Tuskegee alone, not the broader pattern.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In her study of the North Atlantic right whale, marine biologist Dr. Asha Patel challenges the narrative that ship strikes are the primary threat. While acknowledging that collisions are a factor, she demonstrates through analysis of necropsy reports that a majority of right whale deaths are caused by entanglement in fishing gear—and that even those who survive entanglement suffer chronic stress that impairs reproduction.

Which title best captures Patel's argument?
options: Ship Strikes: The Greatest Danger to Right Whales
Entangled in Crisis: How Fishing Gear Threatens the Survival of North Atlantic Right Whales
A Complete Guide to Whale Species
Why Whales Should Not Be Protected
questions.explanation: Patel challenges the ship strike narrative, emphasizing fishing gear entanglement as the primary threat, including chronic effects on reproduction. Option B captures this argument. Option A reflects the narrative she challenges.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Astronomer Dr. Chen Wei argues that the unusual dimming of a star known as KIC 8462852 is not caused by an alien megastructure, as some theorists have proposed, but by an uneven ring of dust and debris left over from the recent breakup of a comet in the star's orbit. She cites spectroscopic data showing the dimming pattern is consistent with a cloud of fine particles, not a solid object.

Which sentence provides the most direct evidence for Chen's argument?
options: KIC 8462852 is a star that has exhibited unusual dimming.
Some theorists proposed an alien megastructure caused the dimming.
Spectroscopic data shows the dimming pattern is consistent with a cloud of fine dust particles, not a solid object.
Dr. Chen Wei is an astronomer.
questions.explanation: Chen's argument is that dust, not an alien structure, explains the dimming. Option C provides the direct spectroscopic evidence—the dimming pattern matching fine particles, not solid objects. Options A and B are context.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A botanist claims that a newly identified plant species in Madagascar belongs to the genus *Vanilla* based on its flower structure. Which of the following, if true, would provide the best evidence?
options: *Vanilla* is a genus of orchids that includes species used to produce vanilla flavoring.

DNA sequencing of the plant shows it shares 98% of its genome with known *Vanilla* species, and its flower has the distinctive pollinia structure unique to that genus, which is not found in any other group of orchids.
The plant was discovered in a remote rainforest.
Madagascar is home to many unique plant species.
questions.explanation: The claim concerns genus classification. Option B provides the strongest evidence—molecular (DNA sequencing) and morphological (distinctive pollinia structure unique to the genus). Options A, C, and D provide context but not evidence specific to this plant.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Tree Species} & \text{Growth Rate (cm/year)} & \text{Lifespan (years)} \\ \hline \text{Balsa} & 600 & 40 \\ \hline \text{Douglas Fir} & 60 & 500 \\ \hline \text{Bristlecone Pine} & 2 & 5,000 \\ \hline \text{Coastal Redwood} & 90 & 2,000 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

Which conclusion is best supported by the data?
options: Faster-growing trees tend to have shorter lifespans among the species listed.
Balsa is the best tree for all construction purposes.
All trees grow at similar rates.
Bristlecone pines grow faster than redwoods.
questions.explanation: Balsa (fastest at 600 cm/year) lives only 40 years, while Bristlecone Pine (slowest at 2 cm/year) lives 5,000 years. The data shows an inverse relationship. Option A is supported. B is subjective. C is false. D is false (2 vs. 90).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Sociologist Dr. Fatima Diop argues that the dramatic decline in crime rates in Medellín, Colombia, was not primarily the result of increased policing, as official accounts emphasize, but of investments in public libraries, cable cars connecting hillside neighborhoods to the city center, and after-school programs that provided alternatives to gang recruitment.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|}\hline \text{Year} & \text{Homicide Rate (per 100,000)} & \text{Public Libraries Opened} \\ \hline 2002 & 184 & 2 \\ \hline 2007 & 65 & 8 \\ \hline 2012 & 38 & 14 \\ \hline 2017 & 23 & 18 \\ \hline\end{array}$$

How does the data relate to Diop's argument?
options: The data contradicts the argument by showing homicides declined.
The data supports the argument by showing a strong negative correlation between library openings and homicide rates.
The data is irrelevant because it does not measure policing levels.
The data suggests that libraries caused the decline in crime definitively.
questions.explanation: Diop argues social investment, not policing, drove crime reduction. The table supports a correlation: as libraries opened (2 to 18), homicides dropped (184 to 23). Option D overstates by claiming definitive causation, but the correlation supports Diop's argument.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A materials scientist argues that a new polymer coating will prevent rust on metal surfaces exposed to seawater. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen this argument?
options: Rust costs the shipping industry billions of dollars annually.

In accelerated corrosion testing simulating 10 years of saltwater exposure, metal panels with the coating showed zero rust formation, while uncoated control panels lost an average of 40% of their structural integrity, and the results were replicated by two independent laboratories.
The polymer coating is transparent and does not alter the appearance of the metal.
Seawater is highly corrosive to most metals.
questions.explanation: The argument claims the coating prevents rust. Option B provides the strongest evidence—rigorous testing simulating 10 years with zero rust, an active control comparison, and independent replication. Options A and D provide context, and C is about appearance, not effectiveness.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The laboratory's centrifuge had been making an odd sound for weeks—a faint, high-pitched whine that the technicians had learned to ignore. When the safety inspector arrived for her annual review, she paused beside the machine and tilted her head. 'When did that start?' she asked. The technicians exchanged glances. 'A few weeks ago,' one said. She wrote something on her clipboard. 'Then you've been lucky,' she said. 'Another few days and this rotor would have failed.'

What can reasonably be inferred about the technicians' response to the sound?
options: They had correctly diagnosed the problem and ordered replacement parts.
They had normalized a warning sign that the inspector immediately recognized as dangerous.
They had reported the sound to management and been ignored.
The sound was a normal part of the centrifuge's operation.
questions.explanation: The technicians had 'learned to ignore' the sound, but the inspector immediately recognized it as a sign of imminent rotor failure. This suggests the technicians normalized what was actually a dangerous warning. Options A and D are contradicted, and C is not supported.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
When author Rachel Carson wrote *Silent Spring*, she did not present herself as an activist. She was a marine biologist by training, and her book is meticulously researched—chapter after chapter of documented cases, chemical analyses, and correspondence with scientists. Yet the response from the chemical industry was immediate and vitriolic. They attacked her not for getting the science wrong but for being a woman who dared to write about science at all. Reading the industry's response now, what is most striking is not the substance of the attacks but their tone—a barely concealed fury that an audience existed for a woman's voice in a debate the industry considered its own.

Which best describes the author's perspective on the industry's response to Carson?
options: The industry's scientific criticisms were valid and well-founded.
The industry's response was driven less by science than by gendered hostility toward a woman speaking with authority in a male-dominated domain.
Carson's research was fundamentally flawed.
The chemical industry welcomed Carson's contributions to the debate.
questions.explanation: The passage notes the industry attacked Carson 'not for getting the science wrong but for being a woman who dared to write about science at all,' and the response displayed 'barely concealed fury that an audience existed for a woman's voice.' Option B captures this gendered critique.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
For decades, the city of Los Angeles drew the majority of its water from the Owens Valley, over 200 miles away. The aqueduct that carried this water transformed Los Angeles into a metropolis but turned the Owens Valley into a dust bowl. In 2006, after years of litigation by valley residents and environmental groups, the city agreed to restore water to the lower Owens River. By 2012, the river was flowing again, and within five years, migratory birds had returned to the valley, and native vegetation was reestablishing itself along the riverbanks. The restoration, though partial, demonstrated that even long-degraded ecosystems can begin to heal when given the chance.

Which best describes the text structure?
options: A comparison of Los Angeles to other cities
A problem-solution structure: environmental degradation caused by water diversion, addressed through litigation and restoration, resulting in ecological recovery
An argument that all aqueducts should be dismantled
A history of California politics
questions.explanation: The passage follows a clear problem-solution arc: the aqueduct's environmental cost, the litigation and restoration agreement, and the results—returning birds and vegetation. Option B captures all elements.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The author of *Barbarian Days*, a memoir of a life spent surfing, opens each chapter not with a wave but with a person—a friend, a rival, a mentor. This structure, he explains, is essential to his understanding of the sport: surfing is not an individual pursuit but a communal one, and the waves he remembers most vividly are inseparable from the people with whom he shared them.

What is the author's primary purpose in opening each chapter with a person rather than a wave?
options: To argue that surfing technique is less important than the equipment used
To convey that the meaning of his surfing experiences is inseparable from the relationships that surrounded them
To provide a comprehensive history of competitive surfing
To suggest that his friends were better surfers than he was
questions.explanation: The author states the structure reflects his understanding that 'surfing is not an individual pursuit but a communal one' and that his memories are 'inseparable from the people.' Option B captures this purpose. Options A and D are not supported.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The mayor's speech was ______, acknowledging the city's fiscal challenges while also celebrating the resilience of its residents and the growth of new businesses in formerly vacant downtown storefronts.
options: one-sided
balanced
inflammatory
triumphal
questions.explanation: The speech acknowledges both challenges and successes—a balanced approach. 'Balanced' means giving equal weight to different aspects. 'One-sided' means biased, 'inflammatory' means provocative, and 'triumphal' means exclusively celebratory.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The developer's proposal described the project as 'boutique,' a term that for the marketing team evoked exclusivity and luxury but that for the neighborhood association carried a less welcome ______: the latest wave of gentrification that would price long-time residents out of their homes.
options: definition
connotation
blueprint
mortgage
questions.explanation: The passage contrasts the positive marketing interpretation with the negative neighborhood interpretation. 'Connotation' captures the emotional and experiential associations—'gentrification that would price long-time residents out.' 'Definition' is the literal meaning.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
In architecture, a ______ is a central space in a building, often open to the sky, around which the structure is organized. This design element, common in ancient Roman houses, has influenced public buildings from courthouses to shopping malls, where the vertical open space serves as both a circulation hub and a source of natural light.
options: atrium
facade
portico
cupola
questions.explanation: The definition—a central open space organizing a building, from Roman houses to modern malls—is an 'atrium.' 'Facade' is the front exterior, 'portico' is a columned porch, and 'cupola' is a small dome.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The startup had been bleeding money for its first two years—the founder's savings, then a small loan from her parents, then a grant that was more an act of faith than an investment. When the first check from a venture capital firm finally arrived, she did not frame it or post it on social media. She took it to the bank, deposited it, and went back to work. The money was not a victory lap, she understood. It was permission to keep running.

Which best describes the function of the final two sentences?
options: To suggest that the founder was ungrateful for the investment
To reframe the investment not as an endpoint but as a continuation of the struggle—a resource that enables further work rather than a moment of arrival
To argue that venture capital funding is unnecessary for startups
To provide a literal account of the banking process
questions.explanation: The final sentences interpret the investment as 'permission to keep running'—not a finish line but a continuation. Option B captures this reframing. Options A and C lack support, and D misreads the figurative meaning.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
A researcher has compiled the following notes:

— The Darvaza gas crater, located in Turkmenistan, is a burning natural gas field that has been on fire continuously since 1971.
— It was created when a Soviet drilling rig collapsed into an underground cavern, and geologists set the escaping gas on fire to prevent it from spreading.
— The crater, nicknamed the 'Door to Hell,' measures 69 meters across and 30 meters deep.
— Despite safety and environmental concerns, the site has become a tourist attraction, with visitors camping at the rim to watch the flames.

Which choice most effectively combines the information?
options: The Darvaza crater is in Turkmenistan and has been burning since 1971 after a Soviet drilling accident, with geologists igniting the gas to contain it; today the 69-meter 'Door to Hell' draws tourists who camp at its edge despite the hazards.
The crater is called the Door to Hell and is 69 meters across.
Soviet geologists set the gas on fire in 1971.
Tourists visit the crater in Turkmenistan.
questions.explanation: Option A synthesizes all four notes: the location, the origin story and date, the dimensions, and the current tourist phenomenon. Options B, C, and D each capture only fragments.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The editor of a literary magazine wants to dedicate a special issue to climate fiction but fears that readers may dismiss it as didactic or preachy. Which approach would best attract readers while achieving the editorial goal?
options: An issue filled entirely with essays explaining the science of climate change.

An issue that pairs gripping, character-driven stories set in climate-altered futures with interviews with the authors about why storytelling—rather than polemic—is the best tool for exploring the emotional and moral dimensions of environmental crisis.
An issue reprinting classic nature writing from the nineteenth century.
An issue with charts and graphs showing rising global temperatures.
questions.explanation: The goal is to attract readers while addressing climate themes without being preachy. Option B achieves this by emphasizing gripping stories (appealing to readers) and framing storytelling as a tool for exploring emotional and moral dimensions (addressing the theme). Options A and D risk being didactic.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The poet's first collection was restrained and classical, every poem a sonnet or a villanelle polished to metrical perfection; her second book, ______, erupts into free verse, its lines sprawling across the page in a deliberate rejection of the constraints she once embraced.
options: by contrast,
in other words,
consequently,
for example,
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the restrained first collection with the unrestrained second. 'By contrast' signals this opposition. 'Consequently' would imply the first style caused the rejection of it, which is not the intended logic.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
______ the city had invested heavily in flood barriers after the devastating storm of 2005, the unprecedented rainfall in 2023 overwhelmed the system, and several neighborhoods experienced severe flooding.
options: Although
Because
Unless
Whereas
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts the investment with the system's failure. 'Although' correctly introduces this concessive relationship—despite investment, flooding occurred. 'Because' would make the investment the cause of flooding (illogical).

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The collector had spent decades amassing one of the world's finest collections of early photography; when she donated it to the museum, the curators were astonished by its breadth and quality.
options: NO CHANGE
photography, when
photography when
photography, and when
questions.explanation: The sentence correctly uses a semicolon to join two related independent clauses. Option B creates a comma splice, C creates a fused sentence, and D adds unnecessary coordination.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The internship program teaches participants not only to analyze complex datasets but also ______ their findings clearly to nontechnical audiences.
options: to present
presenting
present
presented
questions.explanation: The correlative 'not only to analyze...but also ______' requires parallelism with the infinitive. 'To present' maintains this structure. 'Presenting,' 'present,' and 'presented' break the parallelism.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The vaccine had been effective in early trials, ______ the emergence of a new variant raised questions about whether the protection would hold.
options: and
but
because
unless
questions.explanation: The sentence contrasts early effectiveness with new doubts. 'But' signals this opposition. 'And' would present the doubts as consistent, 'because' would reverse causality, and 'unless' introduces a condition.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The intricate system of canals and locks that connects the Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean, which ______ a marvel of nineteenth-century engineering, continues to carry millions of tons of cargo each year.
options: were
was
are
have been
questions.explanation: The subject of the relative clause is 'which,' referring to 'system'—a singular noun. The singular past tense 'was' correctly agrees. Options A, C, and D are plural.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
Neither the lead author of the study nor the researchers who collected the data ______ available to comment on the findings before the paper's official publication date.
options: was
were
is
has been
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor,' the verb agrees with the nearest subject. Here, 'researchers' is plural and closer to the verb, so the plural past tense 'were' is correct. Options A, C, and D are singular.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The philanthropic foundation announced that ______ would be focusing its grantmaking on grassroots organizations led by members of the communities they serve.
options: they
it
we
he
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'The philanthropic foundation,' a singular collective entity. The singular pronoun 'it' correctly refers to the foundation. 'They' is plural, 'we' includes the reader, and 'he' is gendered.

questions.question:
(Module 2 English)
The anthology features stories by several emerging writers, but the editor acknowledged that the collection reflects ______ own interest in narratives of migration and belonging.
options: her
their
its
one's
questions.explanation: The antecedent is 'the editor,' singular. 'Her' is the singular feminine possessive pronoun. 'Their' is plural in formal agreement, 'its' is for non-human antecedents, and 'one's' is impersonal.
